summary: Three of four findings cluster around security fix 9dc148f5 (Sensitive masking): the fix was scoped to results.json only, and the two Report tests that should detect regressions on the JUnit path assert on an input-echo scalar instead of file content. Taken together, a sensitive-data leak via junit.xml has no test coverage. The step-propagation and AfterAction-widening seams are clean.

previous_reviews_assessed.codeanalyzer: agree — root-cause fix at SplitAtConditions indexer is correct; remaining _items[i] call sites don't need Step
previous_reviews_assessed.tester: partial — F11 rated minor but masks a real security gap (junit.xml unmasked + content not verified)
previous_reviews_assessed.security: partial — finding #3 fix (9dc148f5) addresses results.json only; junit.xml leak path still open despite being named in the original finding

findings.issue: Test asserts %report.format% which echoes the input parameter — passes even if `case "junit":` is deleted. Combined with IncludesCoverageTables (same pattern), neither verifies file content. These are the only PLang tests for format routing.
findings.impact: JUnit-path regressions (including the sensitive-data leak in finding #2) have no detection. Tester v6 rated this minor; I rate major because it compounds finding #2.
findings.suggestion: Replace `assert %report.format% equals 'junit'` with `assert %report.content% contains '<testsuites'`, and for IncludesCoverageTables use `assert %report.content% contains 'branchCoverage'`.

findings.issue: Security fix 9dc148f5 applies SensitivePropertyFilter.Mask only to BuildJson (line 281). BuildJUnit emits run.Error?.Message via raw StringBuilder — no mask. AssertionError.Message is built via value.ToString() (AssertionError.cs:36-46), which can embed the raw content of an object containing [Sensitive] properties (e.g. Identity.PrivateKey). The original security finding #3 explicitly named both results.json and junit.xml.
findings.impact: CI artefact (junit.xml) may contain unmasked sensitive values from failed-assert error messages. Same threat model as the fixed JSON path.
findings.suggestion: Fix AssertionError.FormatValue (AssertionError.cs:42-47) to serialize non-trivial values via Json.DiagnosticOutput instead of value.ToString(). Benefits console, JSON, and JUnit paths in one place — matches OBP 'behavior belongs to the owner' (AssertionError owns its own formatting).

findings.issue: Unit test Sensitive_DiagnosticOutput_MasksValueAsAsterisks proves the modifier works in isolation. No test asserts the end-to-end path: failing .test.goal with an Identity variable → AssertionError.Variables → report.cs BuildJson → results.json contains '******'. If report.cs:281 reverts from DiagnosticOutput to CamelCaseIndented, all tests still pass.
findings.impact: A regression on the integration point between Variables snapshot and the mask modifier has no detection.
findings.suggestion: Add Tests/TestModule/Report/TestReportMasksSensitiveVariables.test.goal — fixture that fails an assert on an Identity-like object, outer asserts `%report.content% contains '******'` and `%report.content% doesNotContain <actual-private-key>`.

findings.issue: DiagnosticOutput's docstring states 'distinguishing absent / null / redacted matters — the key must still appear'. But SensitivePropertyFilter.Mask strips non-string [Sensitive] properties (line 58 RemoveAt). Violates the stated intent for any future non-string sensitive prop.
findings.impact: Latent. No current non-string [Sensitive] properties exist.
findings.suggestion: Replace the strip fallback with a placeholder ValueProvider that emits a static structural stand-in (e.g. { redacted: '<sensitive>' }).
